assessment_items.question_content:
**What materials are used to create El Anatsui's *Old Man's Cloth*?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/78b2b0c307ea1aa2bdaae58b3dc54091.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In some of his earliest mature works, El Anatsui used an electric chainsaw to slash geometric patterns into wood.

*Old Man’s Cloth* has been constructed from flattened liquor bottle caps and labels that the artist collects near his home in Southern Nigeria. These are tied together with copper wire.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the purpose of the *Sankofa* movement in Africa?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In 1964, many parts of Africa were experiencing a cultural renaissance associated with decolonization movements. Anatsui joined the unofficial “Sankofa” movement, which was invested in unearthing and reclaiming Africa’s rich indigenous traditions and assimilating these with the European-influenced aspects of society. 

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

choices.content: to reclaim African artistic traditions and assimilate them with European aspects of society
to seek political independence for African cultures colonized by European nations
to promote African artists within a globablized contemporary art market
to distinguish contemporary African artists as different from those working in traditional techniques

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which feature of El Anatsui's *Old Man's Cloth* references the social role of traditional textiles in African culture?**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/78b2b0c307ea1aa2bdaae58b3dc54091.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The bottle caps signify trade between Africa and Europe, and reference the relationship of alcohol to African colonization and the slave trade.

The title underscores the social role of traditional textiles in Africa and their own historical role in trade networks.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

choices.content: the fluid movements of the surface
the use of liquor bottle caps and labels
the use of copper wire thread

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does the Metropolitan Museum of Art classify the art of El Anatsui? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Purposefully disregarding the limited categories imposed by Western art history, Anatsui’s practice emerges from a more expanded understanding of art based on both the radical art practices of the late-1960s, and from a vantage point outside of the Western tradition completely.

Anatsui’s metal wall hangings are owned by two different curatorial departments of The Metropolitan Museum of Art: the Arts of Africa, Oceania and the Americas, and Modern and Contemporary Art.

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

choices.content: Arts of Africa, Oceania and the Americas

assessment_items.question_content: **El Anatsui is a contemporary artist living and working in [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints:
Born in Ghana, Anatsui lives and works in Nigeria although his art is exhibited internationally.  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which ideas are referenced by El Anatsui's *Old Man's Cloth*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Anatsui's technical process and installation method raise questions around the Western distinctions between fine art and craft traditions.
Formal elements such as the gold colors and the rippling movement of the surface reference the history Africa's "Gold Coast."

El Anatsui's large scale works recall Abstract Expressionist paintings by New York School artists such as Mark Rothko (below) and Clyfford Still , as well as the vibrant patterns of traditional African textiles.  

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/daf2ca3bb7c114723cccd7c109e9b33b.jpg) Mark Rothko, *No. 14*, 1960

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/69f3e286ddd7aca0f1eba68bc312a2b1.jpg) Kente Cloth, Akan Culture, Ghana

All of these ideas are addressed in Anatsui's work (all of the above are correct).  

**[Read this text to review.](/humanities/global-culture/global-art-architecture/a/el-anatsui-old-mans-cloth)**

choices.content: the use of found objects in art
the distinction of fine art versus craft traditions
